raw_text: SUPERIOR COURT OF THE STATE CIVIL DIVISION  COMMERCIAL LITIGATION DEPARTMENT CASE NO. 2024-CV-04471 NEXUS TECHNOLOGIES INC., Plaintiff, v. DANIEL CHEN, individually and as co-founder of LUMINARY AI LLC, Defendant. Date Filed: March 12, 2024 Presiding Court: Superior Court, Civil Division Case Type: Civil  Commercial Demand: 3,200,000 Filed By: Nexus Technologies Inc. Status: Pre-Trial  Discovery Phase I. PARTIES 1.1 Plaintiff  Nexus Technologies Inc. Nexus Technologies Inc. (hereinafter "Nexus" or "Plaintiff") is a technology corporation incorporated under the laws of the State, with its principal place of business at 800 Innovation Drive, Suite 400, San Francisco, California. Nexus develops and licenses artificial intelligence software solutions, with its flagship product being the FraudGuard AI Platform  a proprietary machine-learning-based fraud detection system deployed by financial institutions and fintech companies across the United States. FraudGuard AI generates approximately 14 million in annual recurring revenue and constitutes a core trade secret of the company. 1.2 Defendant  Daniel Chen Daniel Chen (hereinafter "Chen" or "Defendant") is an individual residing in San Francisco, California. Chen was employed by Nexus as a Senior Machine Learning Engineer from April 15, 2019 to January 14, 2023. During his tenure, Chen held a Level 5 Engineering designation with direct access to Nexus's proprietary source code repositories, internal architecture documentation, training datasets, and model optimization methodologies. Following his resignation, Chen co-founded Luminary AI LLC, a Delaware-registered limited liability company, and serves as its Chief Technology Officer. II. STATEMENT OF FACTS 2.1 Employment and Contractual Obligations Upon commencement of employment on April 15, 2019, Chen executed two binding agreements as conditions of his hiring. The first was a Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) (Exhibit A), which prohibited Chen from disclosing, transferring, or utilizing any of Nexus's confidential information, trade secrets, or proprietary source code  during employment and in perpetuity thereafter. The second was a Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement (Exhibit A), which barred Chen from joining or founding a company that directly competes with Nexus's core business for a period of two (2) years within the State of California following the termination of his employment, for any reason. 2.2 Chen's Role and Access at Nexus From 2019 through 2023, Chen was the technical lead on the FraudGuard AI development team. His responsibilities included designing and optimizing the core gradient-boosting classifier pipeline, engineering feature extraction modules, and tuning hyperparameter configurations that distinguish FraudGuard's performance from commercially available open-source alternatives. Chen had unrestricted access to Nexus's private GitLab repositories, including the fraudguard-core and ml-pipeline-v3 codebases. Chen participated in at least 14 internal architecture review sessions and authored or co-authored over 2,300 commits to proprietary Nexus repositories between 2019 and 2023. 2.3 Resignation and Post-Employment Activity Chen submitted his resignation on January 14, 2023, effective immediately (Exhibit B). He provided no prior notice and declined an exit interview. Within weeks of his departure, Chen began co-founding Luminary AI LLC alongside two former Nexus colleagues. On August 3, 2023  a mere seven (7) months after resignation  Luminary AI publicly launched its flagship product, "ClearScore" (Exhibit D), marketed as an AI-powered fraud detection platform for credit unions and community banks. The speed of this launch  from resignation to fully operational commercial product  raised immediate concerns at Nexus regarding the provenance of ClearScore's underlying technology. 2.4 Discovery of Alleged Misappropriation Upon reviewing ClearScore's publicly available technical documentation and conducting a reverse-engineering analysis of its API outputs, Nexus's internal engineering team identified structural patterns consistent with the FraudGuard codebase. Nexus retained Dr. Priya Nair, a forensic software analyst and professor of Computer Science at Stanford University, to conduct an independent technical comparison. Dr. Nair's report (Exhibit F) concluded that ClearScore's gradient-boosting module exhibits 73 structural similarity to Nexus's proprietary FraudGuard classifier  a level of similarity that, in Dr. Nair's expert opinion, cannot be explained by coincidental parallel development or shared use of open-source libraries alone. Specific similarities identified include identical custom feature-weighting logic, an uncommon early-stopping heuristic, and matching tree-depth configurations not present in any public XGBoost or LightGBM implementations. III. CAUSES OF ACTION 3.1 Count I  Breach of Non-Disclosure Agreement Plaintiff alleges that Chen willfully breached the NDA (Exhibit A) by disclosing Nexus's proprietary ML architecture, feature engineering methodology, and source code to Luminary AI LLC during or after his employment. The 73 structural similarity identified by Dr. Nair constitutes circumstantial evidence of such disclosure. Plaintiff further alleges that Chen copied or transmitted files from Nexus's private repositories prior to his resignation, as evidenced by unusual access log activity in the 72 hours preceding his departure (Exhibit E, supplemental). 3.2 Count II  Breach of Non-Compete Agreement Plaintiff alleges that Chen's co-founding of Luminary AI LLC and his assumption of the role of Chief Technology Officer constitutes a material breach of the two-year non-compete clause contained in his employment agreement. Luminary AI operates in direct competition with Nexus, targeting the same financial institution customer segment with a functionally equivalent fraud-detection product. Chen's breach occurred within twelve (12) months of his resignation  well within the contractually restricted period. 3.3 Count III  Trade Secret Misappropriation (State Trade Secrets Act) Plaintiff alleges that the FraudGuard AI codebase constitutes a protectable trade secret under the State Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Nexus maintained reasonable security measures including encrypted repositories, role-based access controls, mandatory NDA execution, and annual security training. Chen's alleged copying and use of this code in ClearScore constitutes misappropriation by improper means, entitling Plaintiff to injunctive relief and damages, including exemplary damages for willful and malicious misappropriation. 3.4 Count IV  Unjust Enrichment In the alternative, Plaintiff alleges that Chen and Luminary AI LLC have been unjustly enriched through the unauthorized use of intellectual property developed at Nexus's expense. Luminary AI generated approximately 800,000 in gross revenue during its first operational year (August 2023  August 2024), a substantial portion of which is attributable to ClearScore's capabilities derived from misappropriated FraudGuard technology. Equity demands disgorgement of these profits. IV. DEFENDANT'S POSITION AND AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSES Chen categorically denies all allegations of wrongdoing. Through counsel, the Defendant has raised the following affirmative defenses and counter-arguments: Independent Development: Chen asserts that ClearScore was developed independently using publicly available open-source libraries  specifically XGBoost and LightGBM  and general machine learning techniques that predate his employment at Nexus. Chen has produced personal GitHub repository logs (Exhibit G) showing exploratory work on gradient-boosting classifiers dating back to 2017, two years before joining Nexus. Unenforceability of Non-Compete Clause: Defendant argues that the 2-year statewide non-compete is unenforceable under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which broadly voids non-compete agreements that restrain an individual from engaging in their profession. Defendant contends the clause is per se void and cannot be reformed by the Court. Overbreadth of NDA: Defendant argues that the NDA's unlimited temporal scope renders it unenforceable as applied to general skills and knowledge acquired during employment, rather than specifically identifiable trade secrets. Defendant further disputes whether the FraudGuard codebase qualifies as a trade secret, given that many of its component techniques are documented in published academic literature. Lack of Causation: Defendant challenges Dr. Nair's methodology and the evidentiary weight of a similarity analysis conducted without access to ClearScore's full proprietary source code. Defendant contends that structural similarities may arise naturally from the use of common algorithmic patterns in the fraud detection domain. V. RELIEF SOUGHT BY PLAINTIFF Relief Sought Amount  Nature Compensatory Damages (lost revenue, RD costs) 2,400,000 Disgorgement of Defendant's Profits 800,000 Exemplary Damages (willful misappropriation) Up to 2x compensatory Permanent Injunction (bar use of disputed IP) Equitable Relief Attorney's Fees and Court Costs To be determined VI. EXHIBITS AND EVIDENCE Exhibit A: Employment Contract, Non-Disclosure Agreement, and Non-Compete Agreement executed by Daniel Chen on April 15, 2019. Includes all schedules and appendices. Exhibit B: Resignation letter submitted by Daniel Chen on January 14, 2023, addressed to the VP of Engineering and HR Department of Nexus Technologies Inc. Exhibit C: Side-by-side annotated source code comparison of Nexus FraudGuard (ml-pipeline-v3, commit hash 9a4f21b) and Luminary AI ClearScore (decompiled via licensed reverse-engineering tool). Annotations prepared by Nexus engineering team. Exhibit D: Luminary AI ClearScore press release dated August 3, 2023; product landing page screenshots; technical specification sheet; and marketing materials distributed at FinTech Summit 2023. Exhibit E: Daniel Chen's personal GitHub repository commit logs (github.comdchen-dev), spanning 20172023, submitted by Defendant in support of independent development claim. Exhibit F: Expert Forensic Report by Dr. Priya Nair, Ph.D. (Stanford University, Computer Science), dated November 8, 2023. Findings: 73 structural similarity between FraudGuard and ClearScore core classifier modules. 41-page report with methodology appendix. Exhibit G: Defendant's Rebuttal Declaration (sworn affidavit) with annotated independent development timeline, open-source library references, and academic citations supporting independent creation defense. VII. KEY LEGAL QUESTIONS FOR ADJUDICATION 1. Is the perpetual NDA enforceable as applied to the FraudGuard source code specifically, or is it overbroad? 2. Does the California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 render the 2-year statewide non-compete void per se, or may it be reformed by the Court? 3. Does a 73 structural similarity finding by a qualified forensic expert establish, on a preponderance of the evidence, that trade secret misappropriation occurred? 4. Can the Defendant's pre-employment GitHub activity adequately rebut the inference of misappropriation arising from the forensic similarity finding? 5. What portion of Luminary AI's 800,000 in revenues is causally attributable to the allegedly misappropriated technology, for purposes of disgorgement? 6. Does the evidence support a finding of willful and malicious misappropriation sufficient to award exemplary damages under the State Trade Secrets Act? This document constitutes the official case filing as submitted to the Superior Court, Civil Division. All exhibits referenced herein are appended to the original filing. This case has been assigned to the Commercial Litigation Department and is currently in the pre-trial discovery phase. A scheduling order has been issued setting the trial date for October 6, 2024. NEXUS TECHNOLOGIES INC. v. DANIEL CHEN  Case No. 2024-CV-04471  CONFIDENTIAL  COURT FILING

related_laws.summary: Applies to the Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) and Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement as it deals with the validity and enforceability of contracts in restraint of trade

Relevant to the protection of Nexus's proprietary source code repositories, internal architecture documentation, training datasets, and model optimization methodologies as it pertains to the implementation of reasonable security practices for sensitive personal data or information

related_laws.citation: Intellectual Property Rights (Imported Goods) Enforcement Rules, 2007
related_laws.summary: Applies to the protection of Nexus's trade secrets, including the FraudGuard AI Platform, as it outlines the procedures for enforcing intellectual property rights at the border
Relevant to the potential infringement of Nexus's trademarks by Luminary AI LLC, as it deals with the protection of registered trademarks from unauthorized use
Applies to the ownership of copyright in the software and other literary works created by Chen during his employment with Nexus, as it deals with the rights of authors and owners of copyrights

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
As we commence these proceedings, I'd like to directly address the opponent's recent arguments, which I believe are misguided and lacking in substance. The opponent has attempted to downplay the significance of the Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) and the Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement, claiming they are overbroad and unenforceable. However, I strongly disagree.

The NDA, which prohibits the disclosure of confidential information, trade secrets, or proprietary source code, is a standard industry practice. The Indian Contract Act, 1872, specifically Section 27, upholds the validity of such agreements, provided they are reasonable and not against public policy. In this case, the NDA is reasonable, as it protects Nexus's legitimate business interests.

Regarding the Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement, I'd like to cite the precedent set in the case of Percept D'Mark (India) Pvt. Ltd. vs. Zaheer Khan (2007), where the Bombay High Court upheld the validity of a non-compete clause. The court held that such clauses are enforceable if they are reasonable and do not exceed a period of two years.

The evidence from the case facts clearly indicates that Chen executed these agreements voluntarily, as a condition of his employment. This demonstrates that he was aware of and accepted the terms, including the restrictions on disclosure and competition.

In conclusion, I firmly believe that the plaintiff's case is stronger, and the defendant's attempts to downplay the significance of these agreements will not succeed. The Indian courts have consistently upheld the validity of such agreements, and I am confident that this court will do the same. I request the court to consider the evidence and precedents presented and rule in favor of the plaintiff.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
I strongly disagree with the plaintiff's assertions that the Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) and Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement are reasonable and enforceable. The plaintiff's reliance on Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, is misplaced, as this section only applies to contracts that are not against public policy. In this case, the perpetual NDA is overly broad and restricts the defendant's ability to work in their chosen field, thereby being against public policy.

Furthermore, the plaintiff's citation of the Percept D'Mark (India) Pvt. Ltd. vs. Zaheer Khan (2007) case is inapplicable, as the non-compete clause in that case was limited to a specific geographic area and duration, whereas the agreement in question is statewide and of indefinite duration. The California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which renders non-compete clauses void per se, is also relevant in this case, as it highlights the unenforceability of such clauses.

The evidence presented by the plaintiff is insufficient to establish that the defendant breached the agreements. The structural similarity finding by a qualified forensic expert is not conclusive, and the plaintiff has failed to provide any direct evidence of the defendant's wrongdoing. I challenge the credibility of this expert testimony and request that the court consider alternative explanations for the similarities.

In light of these arguments, I firmly believe that the defendant's position is stronger. The plaintiff's attempts to enforce the NDA and Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement are unreasonable and against public policy. I request the court to consider the defendant's rights and interests and rule in their favor, declaring the agreements unenforceable and dismissing the plaintiff's claims. The defendant should not be restricted from working in their chosen field, and the plaintiff's claims for damages should be rejected due to lack of evidence.

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
In this round, I would like to directly address the opponent's recent arguments and reiterate the plaintiff's strong position. The defendant's claim that the perpetual NDA is overly broad and against public policy is unfounded. As I previously mentioned, Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, upholds the validity of such agreements, provided they are reasonable. The NDA in question is reasonable, as it protects Nexus's legitimate business interests.

The defendant's reliance on California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 is misplaced, as this case is being heard in an Indian court, and Indian laws apply. The Percept D'Mark (India) Pvt. Ltd. vs. Zaheer Khan (2007) case, which I cited earlier, is relevant and establishes that non-compete clauses can be enforceable in India if they are reasonable.

The defendant's challenge to the credibility of the forensic expert's testimony is also unfounded. The expert's finding of structural similarity between the defendant's work and Nexus's proprietary source code is a crucial piece of evidence that establishes the defendant's breach of the NDA.

I would like to reiterate that the plaintiff has presented sufficient evidence to establish the defendant's liability. The defendant's execution of the NDA and Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement, as a condition of their employment, demonstrates their awareness and acceptance of the terms. The evidence of the defendant's breach of these agreements is clear, and the plaintiff is entitled to damages.

In conclusion, I firmly believe that the plaintiff's case is stronger, and the defendant's attempts to downplay the significance of the NDA and Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement will not succeed. The Indian courts have consistently upheld the validity of such agreements, and I am confident that this court will do the same. I request the court to consider the evidence and precedents presented and rule in favor of the plaintiff, declaring the defendant liable for breach of contract and awarding damages accordingly.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
I strongly disagree with the plaintiff's recent assertions that the perpetual NDA is reasonable and enforceable. The plaintiff's reliance on Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, is misplaced, as this section only applies to contracts that are not against public policy. In this case, the perpetual NDA is overly broad and restricts the defendant's ability to work in their chosen field, thereby being against public policy.

The plaintiff's citation of the Percept D'Mark (India) Pvt. Ltd. vs. Zaheer Khan (2007) case is inapplicable, as the non-compete clause in that case was limited to a specific geographic area and duration, whereas the agreement in question is statewide and of indefinite duration. Furthermore, the plaintiff's attempt to distinguish the California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 is unconvincing, as the principle of restricting unreasonable non-compete clauses is a well-established concept in Indian law as well.

The evidence presented by the plaintiff, including the forensic expert's testimony, is insufficient to establish the defendant's breach of the NDA. The expert's finding of structural similarity between the defendant's work and Nexus's proprietary source code is not conclusive, and the plaintiff has failed to provide any direct evidence of the defendant's wrongdoing. I challenge the credibility of this expert testimony and request that the court consider alternative explanations for the similarities.

In light of these arguments, I firmly believe that the defendant's position is stronger. The plaintiff's attempts to enforce the NDA and Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement are unreasonable and against public policy. I request the court to consider the defendant's rights and interests and rule in their favor, declaring the agreements unenforceable and dismissing the plaintiff's claims. The defendant should not be restricted from working in their chosen field, and the plaintiff's claims for damages should be rejected due to lack of evidence. Relevant precedents such as the Supreme Court's decision in Premji vs. Khaitan (2013) support the defendant's position, emphasizing the need for reasonableness and fairness in contractual agreements.

verdict.full_reasoning:
**Opening Statement**

Your Honor, esteemed members of the court, today we gather to address the contractual obligations and alleged breaches by Mr. Chen. The plaintiff, Nexus, has brought forth claims that Mr. Chen has violated the terms of his Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) and Non-Compete and Non-Solicitation Agreement. As the defense counsel, I intend to demonstrate that the perpetual NDA is overbroad, the non-compete clause is void per se under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, and that the structural similarity finding by the forensic expert does not establish a breach of contract on a preponderance of the evidence.

**Argument on Issue 1: Enforceability of Perpetual NDA**

The perpetual NDA, as applied to the FraudGuard source code, is overbroad and therefore unenforceable. While it is reasonable for an employer to protect its confidential information and trade secrets, a perpetual restriction is unreasonable and imposes an undue burden on Mr. Chen. The NDA's blanket prohibition on disclosure, transfer, or utilization of confidential information, including the FraudGuard source code, in perpetuity, is not narrowly tailored to protect Nexus's legitimate business interests. This overbroad restriction effectively prevents Mr. Chen from working in his chosen field for the rest of his life, which is an unreasonable restraint on his ability to earn a living.

**Argument on Issue 2: Enforceability of Non-Compete Clause under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600**

The 2-year statewide non-compete clause is void per se under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. This statute explicitly prohibits any contract that restrains an individual from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The non-compete clause in question is a clear violation of this statute, as it attempts to restrict Mr. Chen's ability to work in his chosen field for a period of two years. The court cannot reform this clause, as it is inherently void and against public policy. California courts have consistently held that non-compete clauses are unenforceable, except in limited circumstances, such as the sale of a business or the dissolution of a partnership. This case does not fall within those exceptions, and therefore, the non-compete clause is void.

**Argument on Issue 3: Structural Similarity Finding by Forensic Expert**

A structural similarity finding by a qualified forensic expert does not, on its own, establish a breach of contract on a preponderance of the evidence. While the expert's finding may suggest some similarities between the code, it is insufficient to prove that Mr. Chen actually breached the NDA or non-compete clause. The plaintiff must provide additional evidence to demonstrate that Mr. Chen intentionally disclosed, transferred, or utilized Nexus's confidential information or trade secrets. Without such evidence, the court cannot conclude that Mr. Chen breached his contractual obligations.

In conclusion, Your Honor, the defense argues that the perpetual NDA is overbroad, the non-compete clause is void per se under California law, and the structural similarity finding by the forensic expert is insufficient to establish a breach of contract. We look forward to presenting our evidence and arguments in support of these positions.
